plainMeaning: This section confesses the eighth lay vow — to avoid pointless harm — naming its four kinds (evil brooding, heedless play, handing over weapons, and sinful advice) and then purifying its five fine transgressions.
recitation: āṭhamuṁ anartha daṇḍa viramaṇa-vrata caturvidha — avajjhāṇā-carie, pamāyāyarie, hiṁsappayāṇe, pāva-kammovaese. (1) ārta-dhyāna, raudra-dhyāna antarmuhūrta upaharuṁ dharyuṁ hoy, ane vayara, kalaha-vāda, veḍhi, abolā, rūṣaṇāṁ, sarāpa, gāla, karkaśa-vacana-taṇuṁ dhyāna te apadhyānācarita. (2) tathā jīlaṇuṁ, chāṭaṇuṁ, hīṁcavuṁ, ghūta, hoḍa, vāda, vikathādika, hāsya-rasa, puṇya-prabhāvanāṅga ṭāḷī nāṭaka, gīta, phāga, cācara, khelavāḍī, veśa karāvavā ane malla, mahiṣa, śvāna, kurkuṭa jhūjhāḍavā, roga, śrama mūkī sarva-rātre sūi rahevuṁ, āleṁ phūla, phala, pāna-taṇuṁ toḍavuṁ, dehare, pośāle tāmbūlādika daśa āśātanā-taṇuṁ karavuṁ, te pramādācarita. (3) tathā dākṣiṇā-pākhe kośa, kodāḷo, pāvaḍo, dhanuṣa, khaḍga, āga āpīe te hiṁsā-pradāna. (4) tathā ghoḍā, kalhoḍā samarāva, khetra kheḍa, gāḍuṁ vāha, hāṭa māṇḍa isyuṁ je bolīe te pāpopadeśa kahīe. e anartha-daṇḍa caturvidha je pariharīe te āṭhamā anartha daṇḍa viramaṇa-vrata-taṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. kandappe, kukkuie, moharie, saṁjuttādigaraṇo, uvabhoga-paribhoga-aïrege. kandarpa-lage sa-vikāra vacana bolyāṁ hoy. kaukucya — bhāṇḍa-ceṣṭā, mukha, nayana-vikāra karī, loka hasāvyā hoy. maukharya — vācāla-paṇe pīyārī tāṁtī, rāḍhi-marma, moso bolyo hoy. saṁyuktādhikaraṇa — ukhala, mūśala, ghaṇṭī, ghāṇī, nisāha, lohuṁ, dhanuṣya, bāṇa, jotra parāṇo melī mehlyāṁ hoy. snāne, bhojane, paherave, oḍhave, atyāsakti kīdhī hoy, tathā rātre māthuṁ guṁthyuṁ, guṁthāvyuṁ hoy. vāsī-gāre līṁpaṇuṁ karyuṁ, karāvyuṁ hoy. nikkaṁśa-vacana — asatya vacana bolyāṁ hoy. aneruṁ āṭhamā anartha daṇḍa viramaṇa-vrata viṣe.

wordByWord.notes: Marks the third guṇavrata in the twelve-vow scheme.

wordByWord.gloss: the vow of desisting from purposeless harm
wordByWord.notes: Daṇḍa here = self-inflicted karmic harm; anartha = without need or gain.
Announces the four heads named immediately.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ava-jjhāṇa < Skt. apa-dhyāna 'ill meditation' + ācarita 'engaging in'
wordByWord.notes: First of the four; locative/instrumental Prakrit formula form.
Second head.

wordByWord.gloss: by handing over means of violence (hiṁsā-pradāna)
wordByWord.notes: Third head.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pāva < Skt. pāpa + kamma < karma + uvaesa < upadeśa 'instruction'
wordByWord.notes: Fourth head; the four named here are then each defined.
First of the two inauspicious dhyānas.
Second inauspicious dhyāna.

wordByWord.gloss: for under a muhūrta (a brief span)
wordByWord.notes: The minimum sustained duration that makes a brooding a fault.

wordByWord.gloss: may have held/entertained near at hand
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. upahara 'bringing near' + Guj. dharvuṁ 'to hold' + hoy 'may be'
wordByWord.notes: Confessional optative: 'if I have...'.
Begins the list of mental objects brooded upon.
Sense uncertain; likely a persisting wrap of ill-feeling.
A familiar domestic grudge.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ruṣ 'to be angry' > Guj. rūsaṇāṁ

wordByWord.notes: Names the act that ties the whole list into apadhyāna.

wordByWord.gloss: that is conduct of evil brooding
wordByWord.notes: Closes the first head.
Connector opening each subsequent head.
Begins idle-amusement list under pramāda.
The four vikathās: women, food, country, king.

wordByWord.gloss: setting aside acts that promote merit and the faith
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. puṇya 'merit' + prabhāvanā 'propagation' + aṅga 'limb' + Guj. ṭāḷvuṁ 'to exclude'
wordByWord.notes: Key qualifier: only non-meritorious spectacle is faulted; dharma-prabhāvanā is exempt.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. veṣa 'attire' + Guj. karāvavuṁ 'to have done'

wordByWord.notes: Begins the animal/man-baiting list.
Bull/cock/dog-fighting as cruel sport.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. roga 'disease' + śrama 'exertion' + Guj. mūkvuṁ 'to set aside'
wordByWord.notes: Sleeping all night is faulted only when not from sickness or genuine tiredness.

wordByWord.gloss: to lie sleeping the whole night (in sloth)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sarva 'all' + rātri 'night' + Guj. sūvuṁ 'to sleep'

wordByWord.gloss: needlessly plucking fresh flowers, fruit and leaves
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. āluṁ 'fresh/moist' + phūla 'flower' + phala 'fruit' + pāna 'leaf' + toḍavuṁ 'to break'
wordByWord.notes: Harm to vegetal (one-sensed) life for no purpose.

wordByWord.gloss: in the temple, in the pauṣadha-hall
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. deharuṁ 'shrine' < Skt. devagṛha; pośāḷa < pauṣadha-śālā
wordByWord.notes: Sets the scene for the ten disrespects.
Example of temple āśātanā.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. daśa 'ten' + āśātanā 'irreverence' + Guj. karavuṁ 'to do'
wordByWord.notes: The ten standard temple/āśātanā lapses.
Closes the second head.

wordByWord.gloss: out of social obligation/to oblige someone
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. dākṣiṇya 'courtesy' + Guj. pākhe 'on the side of'
wordByWord.notes: Motive that does not excuse handing over weapons.
Begins the implements-of-harm list.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. agni > Guj. āga 'fire' + āpavuṁ 'to give'

wordByWord.gloss: that is the handing-over of violence
wordByWord.notes: Closes the third head.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ghoḍo 'horse' + kalhoḍā 'steeds/mares(?)' + samaravuṁ 'to make ready'
wordByWord.notes: Examples of urging worldly toil; kalhoḍā sense uncertain.

wordByWord.token: ઇસ્યું જે બોલીએ તે પાપોપદેશ કહીએ
wordByWord.translit: isyuṁ je bolīe te pāpopadeśa kahīe
wordByWord.gloss: such things as one says — that is called sinful advising
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. isyuṁ 'such' + bolavuṁ 'to speak'; Skt. pāpa + upadeśa
wordByWord.notes: Closes the fourth head.

wordByWord.gloss: whoever forsakes this four-fold purposeless harm
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pari-√hṛ 'to give up' > Guj. pariharavuṁ

wordByWord.gloss: I now purify the five transgressions
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pañca 'five' + atiyāra (this book's spelling of atichāra) + Guj. śodhavuṁ 'to cleanse'
wordByWord.notes: Standard refrain introducing the five aticāras.
First aticāra; Prakrit formula form.
Second aticāra.
Third aticāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṁjutta < Skt. saṁyukta 'joined' + ahigaraṇa < adhikaraṇa 'instrument of harm'
wordByWord.notes: Fourth aticāra; = asamīkṣyādhikaraṇa in Tattvārtha 7.27.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit uvabhoga < upabhoga 'one-time use' + paribhoga 'repeated use' + aïrega < atireka 'excess'
wordByWord.notes: Fifth aticāra.

wordByWord.gloss: Under kandarpa — may have spoken passion-stirring words
wordByWord.notes: Gloss of aticāra 1.

wordByWord.token: કૌકુચ્ય :- ભાંડચેષ્ટા, મુખ, નયનવિકાર કરી, લોક હસાવ્યા હોય
wordByWord.translit: kaukucya — bhāṇḍa-ceṣṭā, mukha, nayana-vikāra karī, loka hasāvyā hoy
wordByWord.gloss: Kautkucya — by buffoon-antics and grimaces of mouth and eye, may have made people laugh
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhāṇḍa 'jester' + ceṣṭā 'gesture'; mukha 'face' + nayana 'eye' + vikāra 'contortion'
wordByWord.notes: Gloss of aticāra 2.

wordByWord.token: મૌખર્ય :- વાચાલપણે પીયારી તાંતી, રાઢિમર્મ, મોસો બોલ્યો હોય
wordByWord.translit: maukharya — vācāla-paṇe pīyārī tāṁtī, rāḍhi-marma, moso bolyo hoy
wordByWord.gloss: Maukharya — garrulously, may have spoken provoking taunts, exposed secrets, and falsehood
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vācāla 'loquacious'; marma 'secret/sore point'; Guj. moso/mṛṣā 'lie'
wordByWord.notes: Gloss of aticāra 3; pīyārī tāṁtī, rāḍhi-marma are dialectal — 'needling, stirring sore matters'.

wordByWord.token: સંયુક્તાધિકરણ :- ઉખલ, મૂશલ, ઘંટી, ઘાણી, નિસાહ, લોહું, ધનુષ્ય, બાણ, જોત્ર પરાણો મેલી મેહલ્યાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: saṁyuktādhikaraṇa — ukhala, mūśala, ghaṇṭī, ghāṇī, nisāha, lohuṁ, dhanuṣya, bāṇa, jotra parāṇo melī mehlyāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: Saṁyuktādhikaraṇa — may have left mortar, pestle, handmill, oil-press, anvil(?), iron, bow, arrow, yoke and goad lying coupled and ready for use
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ukhala/ulūkhala 'mortar', musala 'pestle', ghaṭṭa 'mill', ghāṇī 'press', loha 'iron', dhanus, bāṇa 'arrow', yoktra 'yoke'
wordByWord.notes: Gloss of aticāra 4: leaving harm-instruments assembled invites others to do violence with them.

wordByWord.token: સ્નાને, ભોજને, પહેરવે, ઓઢવે, અત્યાસક્તિ કીધી હોય
wordByWord.translit: snāne, bhojane, paherave, oḍhave, atyāsakti kīdhī hoy
wordByWord.gloss: may have indulged excessive attachment in bathing, eating, wearing and wrapping
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. snāna 'bath', bhojana 'food', ati-āsakti 'over-attachment'; Guj. paheravuṁ/oḍhavuṁ 'to wear/cover'
wordByWord.notes: Illustrates aticāra 5 (upabhoga-paribhoga excess).

wordByWord.token: તથા રાત્રે માથું ગુંથ્યું, ગુંથાવ્યું હોય
wordByWord.translit: tathā rātre māthuṁ guṁthyuṁ, guṁthāvyuṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: and at night may have braided, or had braided, the hair
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. rātri 'night'; Guj. māthuṁ 'head/hair' + guṁthavuṁ 'to plait'
wordByWord.notes: Night grooming counted as needless harm/heedlessness.

wordByWord.gloss: may have plastered, or had plastered, with stale cow-dung
wordByWord.notes: Stale dung breeds and destroys life — a heedless household lapse.
Reading uncertain; book glosses it as asatya vacana.

wordByWord.token: અનેરું આઠમા અનર્થ દંડ વિરમણવ્રત વિષે
wordByWord.translit: aneruṁ āṭhamā anartha daṇḍa viramaṇa-vrata viṣe
wordByWord.gloss: and whatever else regarding the eighth vow of desisting from purposeless harm
wordByWord.notes: The sweep-up clause leading into the unspoken micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ refrain.

literalTranslation: Eighth, the vow of desisting from purposeless harm, is four-fold: evil-brooding conduct, heedless conduct, handing-over of violence, and advice toward sin. (1) Anguished and cruel brooding held for even a brief span, and dwelling on enmity, quarrel, entanglement, sulking silence, resentments, curses, abuse and harsh speech — that is evil-brooding conduct. (2) Likewise splashing, spraying, swinging, dicing, betting, wrangling, idle gossip, buffoonery; and — setting aside what promotes merit and the faith — staging plays, songs, Holi-songs, round-dances, sports, masquerades, and setting wrestlers, buffaloes, dogs and cocks to fight; and — illness and fatigue excepted — lying asleep the whole night; needlessly plucking fresh flowers, fruit and leaves; in temple or pauṣadha-hall committing the ten disrespects such as chewing betel — that is heedless conduct. (3) Likewise, to oblige someone, giving a mattock, hoe, shovel, bow, sword or fire — that is the handing-over of violence. (4) Likewise saying 'ready the horses and mounts, plough the field, drive the cart, set up the shop' — that is called sinful advising. Whoever forsakes this four-fold purposeless harm — for the eighth vow I now purify its five transgressions: kandarpa, kautkucya, maukharya, saṁyuktādhikaraṇa, excess of consumables-and-durables. Kandarpa — may have spoken passion-stirring words. Kautkucya — with buffoonery and grimaces, made people laugh. Maukharya — garrulously spoke taunts, exposed secrets, falsehood. Saṁyuktādhikaraṇa — left mortar, pestle, handmill, oil-press, anvil, iron, bow, arrow, yoke and goad lying assembled. Indulged excessive attachment in bathing, eating, wearing, wrapping; at night braided or had hair braided; plastered or had plastered with stale dung; spoke pitiless/false words. And whatever else regarding the eighth vow.
idiomaticTranslation: The eighth vow guards against harm that serves no purpose at all, and it has four faces. First, the mind's own violence: anguished or cruel brooding sustained even briefly, and the long nursing of enmity, quarrels, grudges, sullen silences, resentment, curses, abuse and cutting words. Second, the violence of heedless amusement: water-games and swings, dice and wagers and wrangling, gossip and clowning; staging shows, songs, Holi-revels, dances, sports and masquerades — all but what truly spreads the Dharma; pitting wrestlers, buffaloes, dogs and cocks against one another; sleeping the night away out of sloth rather than sickness; tearing off fresh flowers, fruit and leaves for nothing; and, in the very temple or hall of fasting, chewing betel and the other ten irreverences. Third, the violence we put into another's hands — passing, merely to oblige, a spade, hoe, shovel, bow, sword, or fire. Fourth, the violence of the tongue — urging others, 'harness your horses, plough, drive the cart, open your shop.' Renouncing all four, the reciter purifies five subtler lapses: lustful banter, buffoon's mockery, idle prattle, leaving tools of harm coupled and ready, and over-fondness in bath, food and dress — including night-braiding and smearing with stale dung, and pitiless, untruthful speech — and whatever else has strained this eighth vow.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the fifteen karmādāna of the preceding section dealt with sinful livelihood — harm taken on for gain — this verse turns to the opposite and subtler danger: daṇḍa that is anartha, self-harm undertaken for no benefit whatsoever. The verse is architecturally precise: it first names the four heads in their Prakrit formula (avajjhāṇā-carie, pamāyāyarie, hiṁsappayāṇe, pāva-kammovaese), then defines each with a cascade of vivid Gujarati examples, and only afterward 'purifies the five atiyāra.' Note the moral logic by which each head is graded inward to outward: apadhyāna is harm done purely in the mind (brooding on enmity and curses); pramāda is harm in heedless bodily play (water-games, blood-sport, plucking living plants, temple irreverence); hiṁsā-pradāna is harm enabled in another's hands (spade, sword, fire); pāpopadeśa is harm provoked by the tongue (urging ploughing, carting, shop-keeping). Two qualifiers reveal the tradition's exactness rather than mere prohibition: spectacle is faulted only when it sets aside puṇya-prabhāvanā (so dharma-festivals are exempt), and sleeping the night is faulted only roga-śrama mūkī — excepting genuine illness or fatigue. The closing five atiyāra are not new sins but the residue, the fine strains that survive even when the gross four are renounced: lustful banter, clowning, prattle, leaving instruments of harm assembled, and over-indulgence in the body's comforts. Their placement — confessed after the definitions — embodies the sūtra's recurring movement from gross to subtle.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Anarthadaṇḍa is the third guṇavrata, a vow that 'guards' the aṇuvratas by closing the side-doors through which violence re-enters a careful life. Its doctrinal pivot is the Jain teaching that bondage (bandha) is governed by intention and heedfulness, not act alone: the same spade is innocent in a needed task but karma-binding when handed over dākṣiṇā-pākhe, merely to oblige. The two inauspicious dhyānas — ārta and raudra — locate the root of anartha in the mind, consistent with the Tattvārtha doctrine that pramāda (heedlessness) is itself a cause of inflow (āsrava). The five atiyāra map directly onto the classical list of Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.27 (kandarpa, kautkucya, maukharya, asamīkṣyādhikaraṇa = saṁyuktādhikaraṇa, upabhoga-paribhoga-atireka), confirming this medieval Gujarati confession as a faithful vernacular elaboration of the canonical scheme. The condemnation of blood-sport, of plucking fresh one-sensed vegetal life, and of leaving harm-tools coupled all rest on the doctrine of universal jīva and the layperson's duty of jayaṇā (careful restraint) toward even ekendriya beings.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited today, this verse asks the practitioner to audit not the obviously sinful but the gratuitous: the grudge replayed in the mind, the hour lost to mockery or doom-scrolling spectacle, the casual lending of something that will be used to harm, the offhand advice that pushes another into violent labour. Its two exemptions teach discernment over scrupulosity — festive art in service of the Dharma is welcome, and rest in real fatigue is no fault. The fifth atiyāra (atyāsakti in bath, food and dress) makes it a check on everyday over-consumption.

sources.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra, Chapter 7 (vows and their transgressions)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming anarthadaṇḍa as third guṇavrata and its five atichāras (TS 7.27: kandarpa, kautkucya, maukharya, asamīkṣyādhikaraṇa, upabhoga-paribhoga-atireka).

sources.title: Anuvrata and the twelve vows of the Jain householder
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the categorisation of the eight vow among the guṇavratas and the four-fold division of anarthadaṇḍa (apadhyāna, pramāda, hiṁsā-pradāna, pāpopadeśa).

sources.title: Jain Agama corpus — Upāsakadaśāṅga and śrāvaka-ācāra commentaries

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: The verbatim Gujarati text of the eighth-vow section, its orthography (atiyāra), and the ten-āśātanā / blood-sport example lists.

notes: Several Gujarati/dialectal tokens are lexically uncertain and glossed by context: 'veḍhi' (likely a clinging grudge/entanglement), 'kalhoḍā' (a class of mounts beside ghoḍā), 'pīyārī tāṁtī' and 'rāḍhi-marma' under maukharya (provoking/needling speech that exposes another's sore points), 'nisāha' in the instrument list (read as an anvil/block), and 'nikkaṁśa-vacana' (the book itself glosses it as asatya vacana, 'false speech'; the form suggests Prakrit niḥkāruṇya 'pitiless'). The book spells the transgression-word 'atiyāra' (with ય), preserved here per the edition's orthography. The fourth atichāra appears as 'saṁjuttādigaraṇo' (= saṁyuktādhikaraṇa), corresponding to Tattvārtha 7.27's 'asamīkṣyādhikaraṇa'. The closing 'aneruṁ … viṣe' clause elides the spoken micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ refrain, supplied by the reciter. wordByWord groups some adjacent list-items (e.g. the instrument string) under single tokens to keep cognate enumerations intact; every word of the source is covered.
